questions.text: A rotating wheel of radius 0.5 m has an angular velocity of 5 rad/s at some instant and 10 rad / s after 5 s. Find the angular acceleration of a point on its rim.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Option A is correct as ωf=ωi+αt.α= (ωf- ωi)/t= (10-5)/5=1rad/s2.Hence all other options are rendered as incorrect.
It is incorrect as per the given formula.Option A is correct as ωf=ωi+αt.α= (ωf- ωi)/t= (10-5)/5=1rad/s2.Hence all other options are rendered as incorrect.

It is incorrect as per the given formula. Option A is correct as ωf=ωi+αt.α= (ωf- ωi)/t= (10-5)/5=1rad/s2.Hence all other options are rendered as incorrect.

questions.explanation: The angular acceleration of a rotating object can be found using the formula α = (ωf - ωi) / t, where ωf is the final angular velocity, ωi is the initial angular velocity, and t is the time interval. In this case, the initial angular velocity (ωi) is 5 rad/s, the final angular velocity (ωf) is 10 rad/s, and the time interval (t) is 5 seconds. Substituting these values into the formula gives: α = (10 rad/s - 5 rad/s) / 5 s = 1 rad/s2. Therefore, Option A is correct. All other options are incorrect as they do not result from applying this formula to the given values.

questions.text: When the aircraft Concorde is moving in a horizontal plane at a constant speed of 650 ms-1, its turning circle has a radius of 80 km. What is the ratio of the centripetal force to the weight of the aircraft? (g = 9.8 m/s2)

options.explanation: Ratio of centripetal force to Weight of plane is MV2/R : MgV2/R : g = (650*650)/(80,000*9.8)=0.54= Answer.

questions.explanation: The ratio of Centripetal force to the weight of the aircraft=mv^2/r divided by mgm*(650)^2/80*1000 / m*9.81=0.54.

questions.text: A car going around a certain curve at a speed of 25 km/h has centripetal force of 100 N acting on it. If the speed of the car is doubled, the centripetal force:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When the speed of the car is doubled, the centripetal force increases by a factor of four because the force is proportional to the square of the speed.
This option is incorrect. The centripetal force is not directly proportional to speed but to the square of speed, so simply doubling the speed results in the force being quadrupled, not doubled.
This option is incorrect. The centripetal force is not related to the square root of the speed. Doubling the speed actually quadruples the force.

options.text: Is reduced to 1/2 of the original value
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Increasing speed cannot reduce the centripetal force. In fact, doubling the speed increases the force significantly.

options.text: Is reduced to 1/6 of the original value
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The centripetal force is not reduced by increasing speed; rather, it is increased. Doubling the speed results in a fourfold increase in force.

questions.explanation: The formula that will be used here will be: F=(m)(v)2/r. By doubling the speed of the car, the centripetal force should be quadrupled due to the power of 2.

questions.text: The rate of change of angular velocity with respect to _ defines angular acceleration.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Speed is a scalar quantity that refers to how fast an object is moving along a path. It does not involve direction and is unrelated to angular quantities like velocity or acceleration.
Frequency measures how often an event occurs over a period of time. While it is related to periodic processes, it is not directly involved in defining angular acceleration.
Distance is a scalar measure of how much ground an object has covered, regardless of its starting or ending position. It is not a factor in determining angular acceleration.

Time is the critical variable over which changes in angular velocity are measured to calculate angular acceleration. It is the correct answer as angular acceleration is defined as the change in angular velocity per unit of time, typically expressed in radians per second squared (rad/s^2).
Gravity is a force that affects the motion of objects, but it is not directly related to the concept of angular acceleration, which is a measure of how quickly the rotational speed is changing.

questions.explanation: Angular acceleration is defined as the rate of change of angular velocity with respect to time. This means it describes how quickly the speed of rotation is changing, and is typically measured in radians per second squared (rad/s^2). None of the other options—speed, frequency, distance, or gravity—relate directly to this rate of change. Speed and distance are linear measures, frequency is a measure of cycles per time but not change rate, and gravity is a force, not a measure of change in motion over time.

questions.text: A body having translatory motion possesses _ and _. In the same way a body having rotatory motion possesses _ and _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect as both translatory and rotatory motions have specific properties associated with them.

options.text: Linear velocity... Linear momentum... Angular velocity... Angular momentum
options.explanation: Correct. Translatory motion involves linear properties - linear velocity and momentum, while rotatory motion involves angular properties - angular velocity and momentum.

options.text: Linear momentum... Angular momentum... Linear velocity... Angular velocity
options.explanation: Incorrect. The properties are mismatched with the types of motion. Translatory motion should be associated with linear properties, and rotatory with angular properties.

options.text: Angular velocity... Angular momentum... Linear momentum... Linear velocity
options.explanation: Incorrect. Angular and linear properties are swapped for the types of motion they should be associated with.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Linear velocity... Linear momentum... Angular velocity... Angular momentum.In translatory motion, all points of a body move uniformly in a single direction, involving linear properties such as linear velocity and linear momentum. This type of motion does not change the orientation of the object.In contrast, rotatory motion occurs when a body rotates around its own axis, involving angular properties like angular velocity and angular momentum. For example, the Earth's rotation about its axis is a type of rotatory motion.All options except B do not properly align the motion types with their respective properties, which is why they are incorrect.

questions.text: When brakes of a car are applied, angular velocity of a flywheel reduces from 900 cycle / min to 720 cycle / min in 6 sec. Angular retardation is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: First, we need to convert the given angular velocities from cycles per minute to radians per second. We know that 1 cycle is equal to 2π radians. Therefore,Angular velocity at the start = 900 cycles/min = (900 x 2π) / 60 rad/s = 30π rad/sAngular velocity at the end = 720 cycles/min = (720 x 2π) / 60 rad/s = 24π rad/sThe time taken for the flywheel to reduce its angular velocity from 30π rad/s to 24π rad/s is 6 seconds. We can use the formula for angular retardation:Angular retardation (α) = (ω2 - ω1) / tWhere ω2 is the final angular velocity, ω1 is the initial angular velocity, and t is the time taken.Substituting the given values, we get:Angular retardation (α) = (24π - 30π) / 6 = -π rad/s^2Therefore, the angular retardation is -π rad/s^2.
Incorrect according to the given solution.
Incorrect according to the solution.
Incorrect according to the solution.
Incorrect. The data given is sufficient.

questions.explanation: This is the following solution.First, we need to convert the given angular velocities from cycles per minute to radians per second. We know that 1 cycle is equal to 2π radians. Therefore,Angular velocity at the start = 900 cycles/min = (900 x 2π) / 60 rad/s = 30π rad/sAngular velocity at the end = 720 cycles/min = (720 x 2π) / 60 rad/s = 24π rad/sThe time taken for the flywheel to reduce its angular velocity from 30π rad/s to 24π rad/s is 6 seconds. We can use the formula for angular retardation:Angular retardation (α) = (ω2 - ω1) / tWhere ω2 is the final angular velocity, ω1 is the initial angular velocity, and t is the time taken.Substituting the given values, we get:Angular retardation (α) = (24π - 30π) / 6 = -π rad/s^2Therefore, the angular retardation is -π rad/s^2.

questions.text: A body is hanging from a rigid support by an extensible string of length L. It is struck inelastically by an identical body of mass m with horizontal velocity v =√2gl , the tension in the string increases just after striking by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option assumes no change in velocity and tension after the collision, which is incorrect due to the inelastic nature of the collision.
This suggests an incorrect calculation of the increased tension, not accounting for the correct post-collision velocity.
Correct: This option properly considers the velocity reduction and force equations after the collision, leading to an accurate calculation of increased tension.
Incorrect: The correct increase in tension is 2mg as calculated through conservation laws and force analysis.

questions.explanation: Initially, the tension in the string is T = mg, which equals the weight of the hanging body. When struck by an identical body inelastically, the system's mass becomes 2m. Conservation of momentum gives us:mv = 2m(v'), thus v' = v/2.Using the inelastic collision principles, the new tension T' is given by T' - 2mg = 2m(v')^2/L. By substituting v' = v/2 and v = √(2gL), we find T' = 3mg.Therefore, the tension increase is T' - T = 3mg - mg = 2mg.Option A is incorrect as it ignores the momentum change. Option B miscalculates the velocity and resultant tension. Option D is incorrect since the calculated increase in tension is indeed 2mg, making Option C the right choice.

questions.text: A body rotates with uniform speed in a circle of radius r and takes time T to complete one revolution. What are the magnitude of the angular velocity ω, the linear velocity v and the acceleration a ?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect option. The correct option is b), where the magnitudes of the angular velocity, linear velocity, and acceleration are w=2π/T, v=2πr/T, a=4π2r/T2, respectively.

questions.explanation: The correct option is b), where the magnitudes of the angular velocity, linear velocity, and acceleration are w=2π/T, v=2πr/T, a=4π2r/T2, respectively.The angular velocity is defined as the angle rotated per unit time. For one complete revolution, the angle rotated is 2π radians, and the time taken is T. (w=2π/T)The linear velocity is defined as the distance travelled per unit time. For one complete revolution, the distance travelled is the circumference of the circle, 2πr, and the time taken is T. (v=2πr/T)The acceleration for uniform circular motion is centripetal acceleration, directed towards the centre, with magnitude given by a=4π2r/T2.

questions.text: A particle of mass m is executing uniform circular motion on a path of radius r. If P is the magnitude of its linear momentum. The radial force acting on the particle is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option incorrectly combines mass and momentum without considering the relationship with centripetal force.
This option incorrectly combines radius and momentum without considering the relationship with centripetal force.

The correct option. The centripetal force is related to the square of the linear momentum and inversely proportional to the radius.The radial force is the centripetal force that keeps the particle moving in a circular path. The centripetal force is given by the following formula:Fc = mv2/r where:Fc is the centripetal force (N).m is the mass of the particle (kg).v is the speed of the particle (m/s).r is the radius of the circle (m).We can write the magnitude of the linear momentum as: P = mv. Substituting this into the equation for the centripetal force, we get: Fc =P2/mr. Therefore, the radial force acting on the particle is P2/mr.Options A, B, and D are incorrect because they do not appropriately consider the relationship between linear momentum and centripetal force in uniform circular motion.
This option incorrectly multiplies mass, radius, and momentum without considering the correct formula for centripetal force.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct.The radial force is the centripetal force that keeps the particle moving in a circular path. The centripetal force is given by the following formula:Fc = mv2/r where:Fc is the centripetal force (N).m is the mass of the particle (kg).v is the speed of the particle (m/s).r is the radius of the circle (m).We can write the magnitude of the linear momentum as: P = mv. Substituting this into the equation for the centripetal force, we get: Fc =P2/mr. Therefore, the radial force acting on the particle is P2/mr.Options A, B, and D are incorrect because they do not appropriately consider the relationship between linear momentum and centripetal force in uniform circular motion.
